figures.caption: Figure 4: Average Frobenius-norm estimation errors for A∗ 0, ∆∗ k and A∗ k versus client number K for Tk = 400 and (ε, δ) = (2, 0.1). Shaded bands indicate the 5th–95th percentiles range across 1, 000 replications.

Figure 5: Average Frobenius-norm estimation errors of federated and single-client methods, and the benefit of federation, versus sample size Tk for K = 5 and (ε, δ) = (2, 0.1). Shaded bands indicate the 5th–95th percentiles range across 1, 000 replications.

Figure 6: Heatmaps of the estimated shared component Â0 and two representative statespecific deviations, ∆̂Wisconsin and ∆̂Illinois, from the federated VAR model under (0.2, 0.05)-DP. Columns are grouped by lag order.

Table 2: RMSFEs of our federated method (Fed-VAR) under three (ε, δ)-DP settings and four single-client benchmarks (Single-VAR) across five states. Within each state, the smallest value is shown in bold and the second smallest is underlined.

Figure 1: Average Frobenius-norm estimation errors for A∗ 0,∆ ∗ k and A∗ k versus the sample size Tk under the single client setting. Shaded bands indicate the 5th–95th percentiles range across 1, 000 replications.
Table 1: Correct selection rate (%) of the ridge-type rank estimator.

Figure 2: Average Frobenius-norm estimation errors of A (n) 0 versus the number of iterations n under different (ε, δ) configurations for Tk = 400 and K = 5. Shaded bands indicate the 5th–95th percentiles range across 1, 000 replications.
Figure 7: Heatmaps of the estimated shared component Â0 and two representative clientspecific deviations, ∆̂AUS and ∆̂USA, from the federated VAR model under NDP. Columns are grouped by lag order.

Figure 3: Heatmaps of average Frobenius-norm estimation errors (upper panel) for A∗ 0, ∆∗ k and A∗ k, and their benefit (lower panel) over single-client estimators, under different (ε, δ) configurations for Tk = 400 and K = 5.

Table 3: RMSFEs of our federated method (Fed-VAR) under three (ε, δ)-DP settings and four single-client benchmarks (Single-VAR) across eight countries (ordered by decreasing sample size). Within each country, the smallest value is shown in bold and the second smallest is underlined.
